problems.prompt: Show `name` and `salary` of employees earning **more than the company-wide average** salary.
problems.hint: Compare against `(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)`.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

problems.prompt: Show `name` and `salary` of employees earning **below** the company-wide average.
problems.hint: Flip the comparison from the previous problem.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

problems.prompt: Who earns the **highest salary**? Show `name` and `salary` — use a subquery, not LIMIT (it should still work if two people tie).
problems.hint: `WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees)`.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

problems.prompt: Who earns the **lowest salary**? Show `name` and `salary` via a subquery.
problems.hint: Same pattern with `MIN`.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary = (SELECT MIN(salary) FROM employees);

problems.prompt: Using `IN` with a subquery, list the names of employees who work in a department located in **Building A**.
problems.hint: The subquery selects `id` from `departments WHERE location = 'Building A'`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE dept_id IN (SELECT id FROM departments WHERE location = 'Building A');

problems.prompt: List names of employees working in **Building B** departments.
problems.hint: Same shape, different building.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE dept_id IN (SELECT id FROM departments WHERE location = 'Building B');

problems.prompt: Which department has **no employees**? Show its `name` (use `NOT IN` or `NOT EXISTS`).
problems.hint: Department ids that never appear in `employees.dept_id`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM departments WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT dept_id FROM employees WHERE dept_id IS NOT NULL);

problems.prompt: List the names of employees who are **not assigned to any project**.
problems.hint: Their `id` never appears in `assignments.employee_id`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM assignments);

problems.prompt: List the names of employees assigned to the **Apollo** project.
problems.hint: Nest twice: assignments for the project whose name is 'Apollo'.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE id IN (SELECT employee_id FROM assignments WHERE project_id = (SELECT id FROM projects WHERE name = 'Apollo'));

problems.prompt: Correlated: show `name`, `salary` of employees earning **above the average of their own department**.
problems.hint: The inner AVG filters `WHERE dept_id = e.dept_id`, referencing the outer row.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees e WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE dept_id = e.dept_id);

problems.prompt: Show `name`, `salary`, and `dept_id` of the **highest-paid employee in each department**.
problems.hint: `salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE dept_id = e.dept_id)`.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary, dept_id FROM employees e WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE dept_id = e.dept_id);

problems.prompt: List project `name`s whose `budget` is above the **average project budget**.
problems.hint: Scalar subquery over `projects`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM projects WHERE budget > (SELECT AVG(budget) FROM projects);

problems.prompt: Using `EXISTS`, list the names of departments that have **at least one project**.
problems.hint: `WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM projects p WHERE p.dept_id = d.id)`.
problems.solution: SELECT d.name FROM departments d WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM projects p WHERE p.dept_id = d.id);

problems.prompt: Using `NOT EXISTS`, find the department(s) with **no projects**.
problems.hint: Same as before with NOT.
problems.solution: SELECT d.name FROM departments d WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM projects p WHERE p.dept_id = d.id);

problems.prompt: Using `EXISTS`, list the names of employees who have **at least one assignment**.
problems.hint: Correlate on `a.employee_id = e.id`.
problems.solution: SELECT e.name FROM employees e WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM assignments a WHERE a.employee_id = e.id);

problems.prompt: List the names of everyone whose manager is **Maya** (look up her id with a subquery).
problems.hint: `manager_id = (SELECT id FROM employees WHERE name = 'Maya')`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE manager_id = (SELECT id FROM employees WHERE name = 'Maya');

problems.prompt: List the names of employees who **are somebody's manager**.
problems.hint: Their id appears in the `manager_id` column.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE id IN (SELECT manager_id FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NOT NULL);

problems.prompt: List the names of employees who are **not** anyone's manager. (Careful: the `manager_id` column contains NULLs!)
problems.hint: With `NOT IN`, the subquery must exclude NULLs — or use `NOT EXISTS`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT manager_id FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NOT NULL);

problems.prompt: Subquery in SELECT: show every department `name` with its `headcount` — including departments with zero employees.
problems.hint: A correlated `(SELECT COUNT(*) …)` in the SELECT list.
problems.solution: SELECT d.name, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees e WHERE e.dept_id = d.id) AS headcount FROM departments d;

problems.prompt: Show every project `name` with `total_hours` — the sum of assignment hours on it.
problems.hint: Correlated `SUM(hours)` in the SELECT list (or a join + GROUP BY).
problems.solution: SELECT p.name, (SELECT SUM(a.hours) FROM assignments a WHERE a.project_id = p.id) AS total_hours FROM projects p;

problems.prompt: Derived table: using a subquery in FROM that computes average salary per `dept_id`, show department `name` and `avg_salary` (rounded to 2 decimals) for departments averaging **above 80000**.
problems.hint: `FROM (SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal … GROUP BY dept_id) t JOIN departments …`.
problems.solution: SELECT d.name, ROUND(t.avg_sal, 2) AS avg_salary FROM (SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id) t JOIN departments d ON d.id = t.dept_id WHERE t.avg_sal > 80000;

problems.prompt: Show `name` and `salary` of employees who earn **more than every Sales-department employee**.
problems.hint: More than every = greater than the `MAX` salary in Sales. Find Sales' dept id with a nested subquery.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary > (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE dept_id = (SELECT id FROM departments WHERE name = 'Sales'));

problems.prompt: Show `name` and `salary` of non-manager-titled employees who still earn **more than at least one Manager**. (Titles containing `Manager`.)
problems.hint: More than at least one = greater than the `MIN` manager salary. Exclude manager titles with `NOT LIKE`.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE title NOT LIKE '%Manager%' AND salary > (SELECT MIN(salary) FROM employees WHERE title LIKE '%Manager%');

problems.prompt: Find the **second-highest** salary in the company: show the `name` and `salary` of whoever earns it.
problems.hint: The max of everything below the max: `salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees))`.
problems.solution: SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary = (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees));

problems.prompt: List the names of employees assigned to **more than one** project.
problems.hint: The subquery groups `assignments` by employee and keeps those `HAVING COUNT(*) > 1`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE id IN (SELECT employee_id FROM assignments GROUP BY employee_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1);

problems.prompt: Which departments have a **total project budget over 100000**? Show the department `name`.
problems.hint: Group `projects` by `dept_id` in the subquery with `HAVING SUM(budget) > 100000`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM departments WHERE id IN (SELECT dept_id FROM projects GROUP BY dept_id HAVING SUM(budget) > 100000);

problems.prompt: List the names of employees in the **same department as Ethan** — excluding Ethan himself.
problems.hint: Look up Ethan's `dept_id` in a subquery; add `AND name <> 'Ethan'`.
problems.solution: SELECT name FROM employees WHERE dept_id = (SELECT dept_id FROM employees WHERE name = 'Ethan') AND name <> 'Ethan';

problems.prompt: Which single project has the **most total hours** logged? Show its `name` and `total_hours`. Use a derived table.
problems.hint: Build `(SELECT project_id, SUM(hours) … GROUP BY project_id)` in FROM, join to projects, order and LIMIT 1.
problems.solution: SELECT p.name, t.total_hours FROM (SELECT project_id, SUM(hours) AS total_hours FROM assignments GROUP BY project_id) t JOIN projects p ON p.id = t.project_id ORDER BY t.total_hours DESC LIMIT 1;

problems.prompt: Correlated: show `name`, `title`, `salary` of employees who earn **strictly more than the average salary for their job title**.
problems.hint: Correlate on `title = e.title` instead of department.
problems.solution: SELECT name, title, salary FROM employees e WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE title = e.title);

problems.prompt: The finale: list the names of employees who work on **every project of their own department**. (Hint: no project of my department exists that I'm not assigned to.)
problems.hint: Double NOT EXISTS: no project `p` with `p.dept_id = e.dept_id` for which no assignment links `e` to `p`.
problems.solution: SELECT e.name FROM employees e WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM projects p WHERE p.dept_id = e.dept_id) AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM projects p WHERE p.dept_id = e.dept_id AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM assignments a WHERE a.employee_id = e.id AND a.project_id = p.id));
